Title: Arabidopsis - a powerful model system for plant cell wall research

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: COBRA family; GPI-anchored protein with cellulose-binding-like motif
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for proper cellulose microfibril orientation; mutation causes primary wall cellulose defects and abnormal anisotropic expansion
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COB was identified from mutants with cell expansion abnormalities. 2. COB encodes a GPI-anchored extracellular protein implicated in cellulose microfibril formation. 3. cob mutants show altered cellulose microfibril orientation and anisotropic expansion defects.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Exact role in polymerization or wall deposition remains unclear.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for secondary wall cellulose deposition; mutation causes secondary wall cellulose defects
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COBL4 is associated with secondary wall cellulose formation. 2. Mutants exhibit secondary wall cellulose defects. 3. Likely acts as a secondary wall-specialized component of cellulose deposition.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Called cob14 in one sentence in review, but Table 1 identifies COBL4/irx6.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes cellulose biosynthesis; mutants show marked reduction in cellulose synthesis affecting both primary and secondary walls
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (kor1/irx2); live-cell imaging of cellulose synthase complex velocity
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KOR1 is a key non-CESA component required for normal cellulose synthesis. 2. Mutations reduce cellulose synthesis and perturb primary wall, secondary wall and cytokinesis. 3. KOR1 likely acts on growing cellulose microfibrils or chain processing/release.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Precise molecular role remains unresolved; hypotheses include primer removal, crystallization control, or microfibril release.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Appears directly involved in cellulose biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KOB1 mutants indicate a direct role in cellulose biosynthesis. 2. Protein localization suggests association with membrane or wall. 3. Molecular function remains unknown.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No recognizable motifs indicating molecular function.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with secondary wall formation and may influence cellulose deposition
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TED6 interacts with the secondary wall cellulose synthase subunit CESA7. 2. It appears to have a general role in secondary cell-wall formation in vessels. 3. A specific function in cellulose deposition is not yet clear.
1. qua2 mutants have decreased pectin content. 2. QUA2 is predicted to encode a methyltransferase affecting pectin biosynthesis. 3. Biochemical activity has not been confirmed.
1. rwa2 mutants show reduced wall acetylation. 2. The gene may influence pectin acetylation or broader wall acetylation. 3. Specific substrate assignment is unclear.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Unpublished result in review; not clear whether it is a specific pectin acetyltransferase.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for GPI biosynthesis affecting AGP maturation; mutation blocks pollen germination and tube growth
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SETH1 is involved in GPI anchor synthesis relevant to AGP maturation. 2. Mutation specifically blocks pollen germination and tube growth. 3. Demonstrates importance of GPI biosynthesis for AGP-related wall biology.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review text describes as homolog of a component in GPI-transamidase pathway.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for GPI biosynthesis affecting AGP maturation; mutation blocks pollen germination and tube growth
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SETH2 is involved in GPI anchor synthesis relevant to AGP maturation. 2. Mutation specifically blocks pollen germination and tube growth. 3. Highlights GPI-anchor pathway requirement for AGP function.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review wording links it to GPI biosynthetic pathway.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Homolog of mammalian mannosyltransferase essential for GPI biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Defect alters cell-wall polysaccharide composition and causes developmental abnormalities via impaired GPI biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PNT1 participates in GPI biosynthesis. 2. Mutation causes broad developmental defects and changes in wall polysaccharide composition. 3. Supports an important role for GPI anchor synthesis in AGP/cell wall biology.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not AGP-specific but affects GPI biosynthesis needed for many AGPs.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains during cellulose microfibril synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential catalytic subunit of primary wall cellulose synthase complex; rsw1 mutant shows reduced cellulose at restrictive temperature
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Temperature-sensitive mutant analysis; loss of rosette structures
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CESA1 is essential for cellulose synthesis in primary walls. 2. rsw1 mutants have decreased cellulose content at restrictive temperature. 3. Defect links CESA proteins to plasma membrane rosettes and cellulose synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point not established; essential catalytic complex subunit
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Other CESA subunits in cellulose synthase complex

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acts as interchangeable position-3 subunit in primary wall cellulose synthase complex to synthesize cellulose glucan chains
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to primary wall cellulose synthase complex as partially redundant subunit
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CESA2 can occupy the third position in the primary wall cellulose synthase complex. 2. It is partially redundant with CESA5, CESA6 and CESA9. 3. Promoter-swapping studies support functional overlap but not complete interchangeability.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Defined mainly by complex composition and redundancy rather than mutant phenotype in this review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes β-1,4-glucan as permanent position-2 subunit of primary wall cellulose synthase complex
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required primary wall cellulose synthase subunit; mutant alleles display cellulose-deficient phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CESA3 occupies a fixed position in the primary wall cellulose synthase complex. 2. Mutations disrupt cellulose-related growth and defense-associated phenotypes. 3. Together with CESA1 and a position-3 CESA, it forms the primary wall cellulose synthase machinery.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Synthesizes β-1,4-glucan as a secondary wall-specific cellulose synthase subunit
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for secondary wall cellulose deposition
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (irx5, nws2); complex localization studies
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CESA4 is one of three non-redundant subunits required for secondary wall cellulose synthesis. 2. Mutation causes secondary wall cellulose defects. 3. All three secondary wall CESAs are required for complex formation and plasma membrane localization.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acts as interchangeable position-3 primary wall cellulose synthase subunit
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes redundantly to primary wall cellulose synthase complex
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CESA5 is one of several interchangeable position-3 CESA subunits in primary wall synthesis. 2. It shows partial redundancy with CESA2, CESA6 and CESA9. 3. Distinct expression profiles suggest tissue-specialized cellulose synthesis roles.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acts as position-3 subunit of primary wall cellulose synthase complex for cellulose polymerization
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal primary wall cellulose synthesis; prc1/ixr2 mutants are cellulose-defective
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; promoter-swap complementation with CESA2
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CESA6 functions as a position-3 primary wall CESA subunit. 2. Mutations cause primary wall cellulose defects. 3. CESA2 driven by the CESA6 promoter can occupy its position but does not fully rescue prc1, indicating partial functional specialization.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Synthesizes cellulose as secondary wall-specific CESA subunit
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for secondary wall cellulose deposition
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CESA7 is a core non-redundant subunit of the secondary wall cellulose synthase complex. 2. Mutation causes secondary wall cellulose defects. 3. TED6 interacts with CESA7, linking it to broader secondary wall formation.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Synthesizes cellulose as secondary wall-specific CESA subunit
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for secondary wall cellulose synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (irx1, lew2, ern1); complex localization studies
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CESA8 is one of the three essential secondary wall CESA proteins. 2. Mutations lead to secondary wall cellulose defects. 3. It acts together with CESA4 and CESA7 in a non-substitutable complex.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acts as interchangeable position-3 cellulose synthase subunit in specific tissues such as pollen/anthers
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely contributes to tissue-specific primary wall cellulose synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CESA9 can occupy the third position of the primary wall cellulose synthase complex. 2. Its expression pattern suggests specialization in pollen and anthers. 3. It is partially redundant with CESA2, CESA5 and CESA6.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Putative cellulose synthase subunit; exact function unclear
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Function remains unclear
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CESA10 is the remaining Arabidopsis CESA family member. 2. It was tentatively designated as position-1-like due to similarity to CESA1. 3. Its experimental function had not been demonstrated in this review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Synthesizes the β-1,4-glucan backbone used for xyloglucan assembly
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Implicated in xyloglucan backbone biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in Pichia pastoris; co-expression with XXT1; co-expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CSLC4 is implicated as a xyloglucan β-glucan synthase. 2. Heterologous expression yields soluble celldextrins, and co-expression with XXT1 produces longer insoluble β-glucans. 3. Coordinated expression with XXT1 supports a role in xyloglucan biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Definitive confirmation awaits characterization of cslc mutants; family redundancy likely.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers xylosyl residues in α-1,6 linkage onto selected glucosyl residues of the xyloglucan glucan backbone
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required with XXT2 for xyloglucan accumulation; double mutant lacks detectable XyG
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant enzyme assay; single and double mutant analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. XXT1 is a biochemically validated xyloglucan xylosyltransferase. 2. It xylosylates cellohexaose in vitro and preferentially modifies the fourth glucosyl residue from the reducing end. 3. xxt1 xxt2 double mutants lack detectable xyloglucan, showing XXT1/XXT2 are essential for XyG biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers xylosyl residues in α-1,6 linkage to the xyloglucan glucan backbone
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts redundantly with XXT1; double mutant with xxt1 eliminates detectable XyG
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. XXT2 is biochemically similar to XXT1. 2. It xylosylates cellohexaose in vitro. 3. Along with XXT1, it is essential for detectable xyloglucan accumulation in Arabidopsis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Single mutants have no major phenotype, indicating redundancy.

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Disruption decreases xyloglucan quantity and degree of xylosylation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. XXT5 is a putative xyloglucan xylosyltransferase. 2. Mutants show reduced XyG amount and xylosylation. 3. Enzymatic activity was not yet confirmed in the review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers galactose in β-1,2 linkage to the third xylosyl residue from the non-reducing end of XXXG, forming XXLG
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for correct xyloglucan galactosylation; mur3 mutants have aberrant galactosylation and reduced wall fucose indirectly
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MUR3 encodes a xyloglucan β-1,2-galactosyltransferase. 2. It specifically galactosylates the third xylosyl residue of XXXG. 3. mur3 mutants lose the proper FUT1 substrate and thus show reduced fucosylation indirectly.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mutant also reveals existence of at least one additional β-galactosyltransferase.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers fucose in α-1,2 linkage onto selected galactosyl residues of xyloglucan
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for terminal fucosylation of xyloglucan; mur2 mutants lack XyG fucosylation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical purification-guided gene identification; mutant lesion analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FUT1 was the first characterized xyloglucan biosynthetic gene. 2. It catalyzes terminal α-1,2 fucosylation of xyloglucan. 3. mur2 lesions eliminate detectable fucosylated XyG, indicating FUT1 is the relevant Arabidopsis enzyme.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Likely sugar nucleotide donor(s) for reducing-end oligosaccharide assembly
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Likely participates in synthesis of the xylan reducing-end oligosaccharide, exact transfer reaction unresolved
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation reduces xylan and the reducing-end oligosaccharide while xylan synthase activity remains unaltered
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IRX7/FRA8 is essential for normal xylan accumulation. 2. Mutants show reduced reducing-end oligosaccharide and heterogeneous chain lengths. 3. Its exact biochemical reaction remains unresolved, but it likely contributes to reducing-end oligosaccharide synthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Likely transfers galacturonic acid in formation of the xylan reducing-end oligosaccharide, exact reaction unresolved
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation reduces xylan and reducing-end oligosaccharide abundance without changing xylan synthase activity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IRX8/GAUT12 is required for normal xylan biosynthesis. 2. Mutants are deficient in the reducing-end oligosaccharide. 3. The protein is inferred to function in oligosaccharide synthesis, possibly transferring GalA.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No direct catalytic proof in the review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Likely transfers xylosyl residues during xylan backbone elongation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for xylan synthase activity and normal chain length
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IRX9 mutants have reduced xylan synthase activity and shorter xylan chains. 2. IRX9 is therefore implicated in backbone elongation. 3. Exact catalytic role remains to be assigned unambiguously.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Originally proposed as xylan synthase; exact function complicated by IRX10 findings.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Essential for xylan synthase activity and likely transfers xylosyl residues in xylan backbone synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation reduces xylan synthase activity and chain length; stronger reduction with irx10 irx10l
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Co-expression analysis; mutant characterization including irx10 irx10l
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IRX10 is essential for xylan synthase activity. 2. Loss of IRX10 reduces xylan amount and chain length, and combined loss with IRX10L intensifies the phenotype. 3. Its exact biochemical reaction remains unresolved but is central to xylan backbone synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Arabidopsis homolog studies did not support prior RG-II assignment from tobacco NpGUT1 analogy.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Functions redundantly with IRX10 in xylan backbone synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances xylan deficiency when combined with irx10 mutation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IRX10L is a close homolog of IRX10. 2. It acts redundantly in xylan biosynthesis. 3. Mutation together with IRX10 leads to a much stronger xylan reduction than irx10 alone.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Likely participates in synthesis of the reducing-end oligosaccharide required for normal xylan formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation reduces xylan and reducing-end oligosaccharide abundance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PARVUS is required for normal xylan biosynthesis. 2. Mutants resemble irx7/irx8 with reduced reducing-end oligosaccharide. 3. It likely acts in synthesis of the xylan reducing-end structure.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Likely transfers xylosyl residues during xylan backbone biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation reduces xylan synthase activity and xylan chain length
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IRX14 is essential for normal xylan synthase activity. 2. Mutants have reduced chain length and xylan content. 3. It likely participates with IRX9/IRX10 in xylan backbone synthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes 1,4-β-linked mannan or glucomannan backbones from GDP-mannose and sometimes GDP-glucose
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Arabidopsis CSLA proteins encode glucomannan synthases; triple mutation of atcsla2/3/9 abolishes stem glucomannan accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical assays of recombinant proteins; single and triple mutant analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Members of the Arabidopsis CSLA family encode (gluco)mannan synthases. 2. Most single mutants show no obvious phenotype due to redundancy. 3. Combined mutation of CSLA2, CSLA3 and CSLA9 completely disrupts inflorescence stem glucomannan accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review treats family-level function; not all individual AGI IDs provided here.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers galacturonic acid in α-1,4 linkage to elongate the homogalacturonan backbone
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes pectin homogalacturonan biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GAUT1 was the first identified pectin biosynthetic enzyme. 2. It is a homogalacturonan 1,4-α-galacturonosyltransferase. 3. Its discovery identified a larger GT8 subfamily likely involved in pectin synthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Forms a complex with GAUT1 in homogalacturonan biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with GAUT1-containing pectin synthase complex
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GAUT7 is part of a complex with GAUT1. 2. The complex is implicated in homogalacturonan synthesis. 3. Direct catalytic activity was not specified in the review.

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: qua1 mutants are pectin-deficient, indicating involvement in pectin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GAUT9/QUA1 is involved in pectin biosynthesis. 2. qua1 mutants are pectin deficient. 3. The mutant also shows pleiotropic effects including xylan deposition defects.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct enzymatic activity not demonstrated in this review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Likely transfers arabinose in α-1,5 linkage during RG-I arabinan side-chain biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal RG-I arabinan side chains; arad1 mutants are deficient in 1,5-α-linked arabinan
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ARAD1 is the first glycosyltransferase linked to RG-I biosynthesis. 2. arad1 mutants specifically lack 1,5-α-arabinan side chains. 3. Heterologous enzyme activity remained to be shown.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Activity remains inferred, not biochemically demonstrated.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: UDP-xylose; endogenous pectin or oligogalacturonides acceptor
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers xylose from UDP-xylose onto galacturonan acceptors to form xylogalacturonan
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for leaf xylogalacturonan accumulation; xgd1 mutants lack detectable xylogalacturonan in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; transient expression in tobacco leaves with transferase assay
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. XGD1 encodes a xylosyltransferase for xylogalacturonan biosynthesis. 2. xgd1 mutants lack leaf xylogalacturonan. 3. Residual silique epitopes imply additional xylogalacturonan xylosyltransferases exist.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Demonstrated biochemically via transient expression in tobacco.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers xylose from UDP-xylose to fucosyl residues via α-1,3 linkage in RG-II A-chain biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Specifically catalyzes a linkage unique to RG-II; single mutants show redundancy
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical enzyme assay; T-DNA mutant analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RGXT1 catalyzes α-1,3 xylosylation of fucose residues relevant to RG-II A-chain formation. 2. Single mutants show no major RG-II composition change due to redundancy. 3. Mutant RG-II acts as acceptor in RGXT assays, supporting the enzymatic assignment.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Double mutant with RGXT2 unavailable due to close linkage.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers xylose in α-1,3 linkage onto fucosyl residues during RG-II biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Functions redundantly in RG-II xylosylation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RGXT2 is a biochemically validated RG-II xylosyltransferase. 2. Redundancy masks strong single-mutant composition phenotypes. 3. Together with RGXT1 and RGXT3 it supports A-chain assembly in RG-II.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Double mutant generation hindered by close linkage to RGXT1.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers xylose in α-1,3 linkage onto fucosyl residues during RG-II biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to RG-II A-chain biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RGXT3 belongs to the same RG-II xylosyltransferase group as RGXT1/2. 2. It specifically catalyzes α-1,3 xylosyl transfer onto fucose-containing acceptors. 3. It supports RG-II A-chain synthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Proline residues in AGP precursor proteins
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates proline residues of AGP precursors in the ER to generate hydroxyproline for arabinogalactosylation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Active AGP prolyl hydroxylase isoform with distinct substrate specificity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. P4H-1 encodes an active prolyl-4-hydroxylase in Arabidopsis. 2. It catalyzes hydroxyproline formation needed for AGP glycosylation. 3. It differs in substrate specificity from P4H-2.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Proline residues in AGP precursor proteins
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates proline residues of AGP precursors in the ER to enable arabinogalactan glycosylation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Active prolyl-4-hydroxylase isoform contributing to AGP maturation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. P4H-2 is an active Arabidopsis prolyl-4-hydroxylase. 2. It participates in AGP precursor hydroxylation. 3. It has substrate specificity distinct from P4H-1.
